title: Textile inclusions in handmade paper
subtitle: Adding threads, fabric scraps, and textile fibres to wet pulp for decorative and structural effects
excerpt: Short lengths of thread, torn fabric, or small pieces of lace or gauze can be added to paper pulp in the vat or sprinkled onto a partially drained sheet. The inclusions become embedded in the finished sheet, visible through the paper or proud on its surface.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), chapter on inclusions and decorated sheets. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/oldpapermak00hunt. Hunter describes thread, fabric, and natural-material inclusions in historical and contemporary practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A material added to the pulp in the papermaking vat before sheet formation, so the inclusion is distributed randomly through the sheet as it forms on the mould.
A material added to the surface of a partially drained wet sheet on the mould, so the inclusion rests on the sheet's top face and is held in place as the water continues draining.

content.text: Paper is made from cellulose fibres. Other fibrous materials, added carefully, can become part of the sheet. A
is stirred into the pulp and distributes itself through the sheet during formation. A

is placed on the wet sheet after forming and pressed in during drying. Both methods produce distinctly different appearances: vat inclusions are embedded and partially transparent; surface inclusions are visible on one face and may create raised texture.

You also need: a prepared vat of dilute cellulose pulp; a mould and deckle; and felts or blotting paper for couching. For inclusion materials: short thread lengths (3 to 15 mm), torn gauze, muslin, snippets of lace, thin yarn, or fine ribbon. Avoid synthetic fibres. Natural fibres such as cotton and linen embed most reliably.
Cut or tear the inclusion material into short lengths, no longer than 15 mm. Longer threads tend to clump or float to the surface rather than distributing evenly.

Stir the prepared pulp vat. Add the inclusions and stir again to distribute them. The proportion should be small: a few dozen short thread lengths per standard A4-size vat. Too many inclusions produce a heavy, uneven sheet.
Form the sheet in the normal way. The inclusions will be randomly placed through the sheet as the pulp settles on the mould wire.
Couch, press, and dry as normal. Held to the light, the finished sheet should show the inclusions as dark threads embedded in the pale paper.
Form the sheet on the mould in the normal way. Allow the water to drain until the sheet surface loses its sheen and the surface pulp stops moving.
Lay the inclusion material carefully onto the wet sheet surface. Use tweezers for fine lace or thread. Press the inclusion lightly into the surface with a damp finger.
Couch and press as normal. During pressing, the inclusion presses into the sheet surface. After drying, it is held at the surface rather than embedded fully.

Fine cotton or linen thread embeds reliably as a vat inclusion. Wool threads swell when wet and can distort the sheet. Metallic or polyester thread floats to the surface and bonds poorly. Fine gauze (cotton muslin or open-weave linen) works well as a surface inclusion, creating a translucent window in the sheet. Thicker fabric swatches are too heavy and cause the sheet to tear when the mould is lifted.
